title: Regenerate Skagit
description: From snow-capped mountains to the Salish Sea, the Skagit River watershed carries water, soil, and stories through one of Cascadia’s most life-giving landscapes.

title: Story of Place
description: The Skagit River watershed stretches from the glaciers of the North Cascades to the Salish Sea, feeding an alluvial floodplain layered with some of the top 2% of agricultural soils in the world. Water, land, farms, forests, and people shape each other here.

title: Indigenous Roots
description: The Skagit Valley is home to the Upper Skagit Indian Tribe, Swinomish Indian Tribal Community, Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe, and Samish Indian Nation. These peoples have lived in deep relationship with the river, delta, forests, and islands for countless generations.
aside: Colonial disruption reshaped these relationships, yet Indigenous presence, knowledge, and stewardship continue to guide what it means to belong to this watershed.

title: Regenerate Skagit's Mission
description: To cultivate bioregional awareness and belonging by inviting people of all ages to see, feel, and imagine life from the perspectives of the watershed—its rivers, trees, animals, farms, and communities.

panels.title: Vision
panels.text: A bioregional culture rooted in the Skagit watershed, where humans live in relationship with land, water, and all living beings to regenerate ecological and social health.

image.alt: Aerial view of the Skagit watershed

panels.title: How We Practice
panels.text: By making art, sharing embodied conversation, Deep listening to land and people, Collective attunement to place

image.alt: Group of people working together outdoors

title: Our Vision
description: A bioregional culture rooted in the Skagit watershed, where humans live in relationship

title: Relationship First
description: To cultivate bioregional awareness and belonging by inviting people of all ages to see, feel, and imagine life from the perspectives of the watershed—its rivers, trees, animals, farms, and communities.

panels.title: Food
panels.text: Weekly potlucks bring people together through shared effort, meals, and conversation.

image.alt: Aerial view of the Skagit watershed

panels.title: People
panels.text: Small projects tackled together—harvesting garlic, tearing down old sheds—build trust through doing.

image.alt: Group of people working together outdoors

panels.title: Land
panels.text: Land is not for us. We are of it. When relationship is restored, life coheres.

image.alt: Flooded Barney Lake with mountains in the background
